content:
# Disagreement Log — Numbers Chapter 5

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 5:2 -- "leper" vs. "one afflicted with skin disease" (tsaru'a)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "every leper" |
| LOGOS | "every leper" |
| PNEUMA | "anyone with a skin disease" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "every leper" -- EMET and LOGOS agree on the traditional rendering. PNEUMA's "skin disease" is medically more accurate (tsara'at covers conditions broader than Hansen's disease), but "leper" is the established formal equivalent from both the Hebrew tradition and the LXX (lepros). The LOGOS textual note rightly acknowledges the semantic range. Retaining "leper" preserves the gravity and ritual specificity of the term without committing to a modern medical diagnosis.

---

### 5:2 -- "unclean by a dead person" vs. "through contact with a dead person" vs. "ritually contaminated by contact with a dead body" (tame' la-nafesh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "every one-unclean by-a-soul" |
| LOGOS | "everyone who is unclean through contact with a dead person" |
| PNEUMA | "anyone who has become ritually contaminated by contact with a dead body" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "everyone who is unclean by a dead person" -- the Hebrew tame' la-nafesh literally reads "unclean to/by a soul" (i.e., a dead person). EMET's hyper-literal "by-a-soul" is opaque in English. LOGOS's "through contact with" adds interpretive specificity not in the Hebrew (the text says la-nafesh, not by touching). PNEUMA's "ritually contaminated" imports a category label absent from the text. The consensus preserves the preposition "by" from the Hebrew le- while making the referent ("dead person") clear.

---

### 5:3 -- "defile their camps" vs. "contaminate the camp" (yetamme'u makhaneihem)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "they-shall-make-unclean their-camps" |
| LOGOS | "they may not defile their camps" |
| PNEUMA | "they do not contaminate the camp" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "they may not defile their camps" -- the Hebrew uses the plural makhaneihem ("their camps"), which EMET and LOGOS preserve while PNEUMA singularizes to "the camp." The plural may reflect the tripartite camp structure (Levitical, priestly, Israelite). "Defile" (LOGOS) is preferable to "contaminate" (PNEUMA) as the standard English rendering of tame' in sacral contexts.

---

### 5:6 -- "sins of mankind" vs. "wrongs another person" (chattot ha-adam)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-sins of the-adam" |
| LOGOS | "any of the sins of mankind" |
| PNEUMA | "wrongs another person" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "any of the sins of mankind" -- the construct chattot ha-adam is genuinely ambiguous: it can mean "sins characteristic of humanity" or "sins against a fellow human." The LXX supports the general reading (hamartiōn tōn anthrōpinōn, "human sins"). LOGOS preserves the ambiguity faithfully. PNEUMA's "wrongs another person" collapses the ambiguity into one reading. EMET's "the-adam" is too raw for English. The consensus retains the broader phrasing, which allows both interpretations.

---

### 5:7 -- "in full" vs. "in its head" (be-ro'sho)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "in-its-head" |
| LOGOS | "in full" |
| PNEUMA | "full restitution" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "in full" -- the Hebrew be-ro'sho ("in its head") is an idiom for the principal amount. EMET's literal "in its head" is unintelligible without annotation. LOGOS's "in full" and PNEUMA's "full restitution" both correctly render the idiom. LOGOS is preferred for being more concise and closer to the Hebrew structure while still idiomatic.

---

### 5:8 -- "ram of the atonement" vs. "ram of purification" (eil ha-kippurim)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-ram of the-atonements" |
| LOGOS | "the ram of the atonement" |
| PNEUMA | "the ram of purification" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the ram of the atonement" -- kippurim derives from kipper ("to make atonement/to cover"), and "atonement" is the standard rendering throughout the Pentateuch (cf. Yom ha-Kippurim = "Day of Atonement"). PNEUMA's "purification" shifts the semantic range toward cleansing rather than covering/reconciliation. The LXX reads hilasmos ("propitiation"), supporting the atonement sense. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 5:12 -- "If any man's wife" vs. "Suppose a man's wife" (ish ish ki tisteh ishto)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "A-man, a-man, when turns-aside his-wife" |
| LOGOS | "If any man's wife goes astray" |
| PNEUMA | "Suppose a man's wife goes astray" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "If any man's wife goes astray" -- the Hebrew ish ish is a distributive doubling meaning "any man whatsoever." LOGOS captures this with "any man's," which is both accurate and natural. PNEUMA's "Suppose" introduces a hypothetical framing not present in the Hebrew conditional ki. EMET's "A-man, a-man" preserves the doubling but is unidiomatic in English.

---

### 5:13 -- "lies with her carnally" vs. "has sexual relations with her" (shakhav otah shikhvat-zera')
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "lies a-man with-her with-a-lying of seed" |
| LOGOS | "a man lies with her carnally" |
| PNEUMA | "another man has sexual relations with her" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a man lies with her carnally" -- the Hebrew shikhvat-zera' ("a lying of seed") is the standard biblical idiom for sexual intercourse. LOGOS's "carnally" preserves the physical specificity in a single word. PNEUMA's "sexual relations" is accurate but clinical-modern. EMET's "lying of seed" is too literal for readable English.

---

### 5:15 -- "grain offering of memorial" vs. "offering of remembrance designed to bring wrongdoing to light" (minkhat zikkaron mazkeret avon)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "a-grain-offering of remembrance, bringing-to-remembrance iniquity" |
| LOGOS | "a grain offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance" |
| PNEUMA | "an offering of remembrance designed to bring wrongdoing to light" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "a grain offering of memorial, bringing iniquity to remembrance" -- LOGOS preserves both elements of the Hebrew doublet (minkhat zikkaron, mazkeret avon) with formal precision. PNEUMA's "designed to bring wrongdoing to light" over-interprets mazkeret ("bringing to remembrance") as forensic exposure and substitutes "wrongdoing" for avon ("iniquity"). EMET is essentially equivalent to LOGOS. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 5:17 -- "Tabernacle" vs. "tabernacle" vs. "Dwelling Place" (ha-Mishkan)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-Tabernacle" |
| LOGOS | "the tabernacle" |
| PNEUMA | "the Dwelling Place" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the Tabernacle" -- ha-Mishkan is the proper name for the portable sanctuary, warranting capitalization. PNEUMA's "Dwelling Place" is an etymologically driven translation (from shakan, "to dwell") but departs from the universally recognized English term. LOGOS's lowercase "tabernacle" treats it as a common noun. The consensus capitalizes to mark it as the named structure, consistent with EMET.

---

### 5:19 -- "under your husband's authority" vs. "under your husband" (takhat ishekh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "under your-husband" |
| LOGOS | "while under your husband's authority" |
| PNEUMA | "while under your husband's authority" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "while under your husband" -- the Hebrew takhat ishekh literally means "under your husband." LOGOS and PNEUMA both add "authority," which is an accurate interpretation of the relational metaphor but adds a word not present in the Hebrew. EMET preserves the bare preposition faithfully. The consensus retains the literal form "under your husband," which communicates the hierarchical sense without importing the interpretive addition.

---

### 5:21 -- "your thigh fall away" vs. "your thigh to waste away" vs. "causing your thigh to waste away" (yerekekh nofelet)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "your-thigh falling" |
| LOGOS | "your thigh fall away" |
| PNEUMA | "causing your thigh to waste away" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "your thigh fall away" -- the Hebrew nofelet is a participle of nafal ("to fall"), not a verb meaning "to waste." LOGOS preserves the Hebrew image precisely. PNEUMA's "waste away" interprets the "falling" as atrophy, which is reasonable medically but departs from the source language. The consensus follows LOGOS, preserving the euphemistic indirection of the original.

---

### 5:22 -- "Amen, amen" vs. "So be it. So be it." ('amen 'amen)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Amen, amen" |
| LOGOS | "Amen, amen" |
| PNEUMA | "So be it. So be it." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Amen, amen" -- the Hebrew 'amen is one of the most universally recognized biblical terms, derived from the root 'aman ("to be firm/true"). EMET and LOGOS retain the transliteration, which has been fully naturalized into English. PNEUMA's "So be it" is a valid gloss (cf. LXX genoito) but loses the distinctive liturgical resonance. The doubled amen is preserved as-is.

---

### 5:22 -- "bowels" vs. "body" vs. "inward parts" (me'ayikh)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "into-your-inward-parts" |
| LOGOS | "into your bowels" |
| PNEUMA | "enter your body" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "into your bowels" -- the Hebrew me'ayikh refers to the internal organs, particularly the intestines/entrails. LOGOS's "bowels" is the closest anatomical equivalent. PNEUMA's "body" is too vague. EMET's "inward parts" is accurate but somewhat archaic. The consensus follows LOGOS.

---

### 5:23 -- "wash them off" vs. "blot out" (makhah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "he-shall-blot-out" |
| LOGOS | "shall wash them" |
| PNEUMA | "wash them off" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "shall wash them off" -- the Hebrew makhah means "to wipe/blot out/erase." In context, the curses are physically washed from the scroll into the water. PNEUMA's "wash them off" best captures the physical action described. EMET's "blot out" is a valid rendering of the root but less specific to the liquid medium. LOGOS's "wash them" omits the directional force. The consensus combines: "wash them off."

---

### 5:27 -- "become a curse" vs. "become an object of cursing" (ve-hayetah le-alah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shall-become for-a-curse" |
| LOGOS | "shall become a curse" |
| PNEUMA | "will become an object of cursing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "shall become a curse" -- the Hebrew le-alah means "for a curse," i.e., her name will be invoked as a byword in future imprecations. LOGOS's "a curse" faithfully renders the Hebrew predicate. PNEUMA's "an object of cursing" adds interpretive specificity ("object of") that, while clarifying, expands beyond the terse Hebrew. The consensus follows the more compact LOGOS rendering.

---

### 5:28 -- "shall conceive seed" vs. "shall be sown with seed" vs. "will be able to conceive children" (ve-nizre'ah zara')
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "and-she-shall-be-sown with-seed" |
| LOGOS | "shall conceive seed" |
| PNEUMA | "will be able to conceive children" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "she shall be sown with seed" -- the Hebrew ve-nizre'ah zara' uses the niphal passive of zara' ("to sow"), meaning literally "she shall be sown with seed." EMET preserves the passive construction and the agricultural metaphor, which implies that conception is a divine gift, not merely a biological outcome. LOGOS's "conceive seed" shifts to active voice and loses the sowing metaphor. PNEUMA's "be able to conceive children" adds a modal ("be able") and substitutes "children" for "seed." The consensus follows EMET's rendering, which preserves both the passive voice and the distinctive biblical idiom.

---

### 5:29 -- "the law of jealousy" vs. "the procedure for cases of jealousy" (torat ha-qena'ot)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "the-law of the-jealousies" |
| LOGOS | "the law of jealousy" |
| PNEUMA | "the procedure for cases of jealousy" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "the law of jealousy" -- torah in its primary sense means "instruction/law," and this is the standard heading formula found throughout Leviticus and Numbers (cf. torat ha-olah, torat ha-minchah). PNEUMA's "procedure for cases of" is an over-expansion that loses the formulaic resonance. EMET's "jealousies" preserves the Hebrew plural qena'ot, but the English singular "jealousy" functions as a category label in context. LOGOS is followed.

---

### 5:31 -- "bear her iniquity" vs. "bear the consequences of her wrongdoing" (tissa' et-avonah)
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "shall-bear her-iniquity" |
| LOGOS | "shall bear her iniquity" |
| PNEUMA | "will bear the consequences of her wrongdoing" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "shall bear her iniquity" -- the Hebrew tissa' et-avonah is the classic formulation for bearing guilt/punishment (cf. Lev 5:1, 17; 7:18; Num 14:34). EMET and LOGOS agree exactly. PNEUMA's "bear the consequences of her wrongdoing" doubles the word count with interpretive expansion: "consequences" for the implied result of avon, and "wrongdoing" for avon itself. The terse Hebrew idiom is well established in English biblical tradition and needs no expansion.
